animals.viewing_tips: Axolotls cannot be seen in any US national park or wild habitat. Visit accredited aquariums such as the Shedd Aquarium in Chicago or the California Academy of Sciences in San Francisco for reliable viewing of captive individuals.

animals.name: Spring Peeper
animals.behavior_notes: Spring Peepers emerge from winter dormancy as temperatures rise above 40°F, gathering in large choruses around woodland ponds, marshes, and wetland edges to breed. Males call persistently at dusk and into the night, producing the iconic high-pitched peeping that signals the arrival of spring.
animals.viewing_tips: Listen for choruses near standing water after warm spring rains at dusk — sound guides you far more reliably than sight, as these tiny frogs are well camouflaged. Use a red-light flashlight and move slowly along wetland edges to spot them calling from low vegetation.

animals.name: Atlantic Salmon
animals.behavior_notes: Atlantic salmon return to their natal rivers in fall to spawn, making them most visible as they leap rapids and pool up in shallow stretches. Numbers in US rivers are critically low, making sightings rare but memorable.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit the Narraguagus or Penobscot River near Acadia in October and look for fish jumping at rapids. Polarized sunglasses help spot dark shapes holding in current.

animals.name: Chinook Salmon
animals.behavior_notes: Chinook salmon, the largest Pacific salmon, migrate upstream in fall to spawn in gravel beds of clear rivers. They are visible in shallow riffles and pools as they hold and rest during their upstream journey.
animals.viewing_tips: Walk streamside trails along the Hoh or Elwha Rivers in Olympic NP in September and October. Look for large dark shapes in pools or fish rolling at the surface at dawn.

animals.name: Sockeye Salmon
animals.behavior_notes: Sockeye salmon turn brilliant red with green heads during their spawning run, making them visually striking in shallow streams. At Brooks Falls in Katmai, they congregate in massive numbers and leap repeatedly at falls.
animals.viewing_tips: The Brooks Falls viewing platform at Katmai offers world-class sockeye viewing in July when fish are densest. Reserve bear-viewing permits well in advance as the area is extremely popular.

animals.name: Coho Salmon
animals.behavior_notes: Coho salmon enter freshwater rivers in late fall and are often seen in smaller tributary streams where they are more accessible to viewers. They are aggressive during spawning and frequently visible near the surface.
animals.viewing_tips: Check tributary streams flowing into larger rivers in Olympic NP in October and November. Look for fish in knee-deep riffles and watch for splashing activity near gravel beds.

animals.name: Steelhead Trout
animals.behavior_notes: Steelhead are sea-run rainbow trout that enter coastal rivers during winter rains to spawn, making them available when most other salmonids are absent. They hold in deep pools and runs and can be spotted in clear water on sunny winter days.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit the Smith or Redwood Creek watersheds in Redwood NP after rainfall events raise river levels. Polarized glasses and a high vantage point above clear pools improve spotting success greatly.

animals.name: Lake Sturgeon
animals.behavior_notes: Lake sturgeon move into shallow tributary rivers in spring to spawn over rocky substrates, making them briefly visible in areas they otherwise avoid. They can be seen rolling or jumping at the surface during spawning activity.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit the Sturgeon River or other Great Lakes tributaries in May and scan shallow rocky runs from bridges or banks. Viewing is best at dawn or dusk when fish are most active near the surface.

animals.name: White Sturgeon
parks: Columbia River Gorge National Scenic Area
animals.behavior_notes: White sturgeon, North America's largest freshwater fish, congregate below dams and in deep river pools during spring, often seen rolling or surfacing. Their enormous size makes them unmistakable when they breach the surface.
animals.viewing_tips: The areas below Bonneville Dam on the Columbia River are a reliable hotspot in spring. Look for large rolling fish from the shoreline or viewing platforms and scan calm surface water at dawn.

animals.name: Paddlefish
animals.behavior_notes: Paddlefish migrate into river shallows and tailwaters in early spring to spawn, making them more detectable in concentrated areas. They filter-feed near the surface and can be seen rolling or finning in calm water.
animals.viewing_tips: Check tailwaters below dams on the Osage and Missouri rivers during spring spawning runs in March and April. Scan calm surface water at dawn for large fish rolling or showing their distinctive paddle-shaped rostrum.

animals.name: Alligator Gar
animals.behavior_notes: Alligator gar are obligate air-breathers that must surface to gulp air, making them uniquely visible from shore or boat during warm summer months. They congregate in warm, slow-moving backwaters and oxbow lakes during summer.
animals.viewing_tips: Scan the surface of slow bayous and oxbow lakes in Big Thicket NP from a canoe or kayak in summer heat. Watch for large torpedo-shaped fish rolling to breathe, which they must do every 30 minutes or so.

animals.name: Manta Ray
animals.behavior_notes: Oceanic manta rays visit warm nearshore waters and reef systems in spring and summer to feed on plankton blooms, often breaching dramatically or swimming in slow circles near the surface. They are most reliably seen during calm, clear-water conditions.
animals.viewing_tips: Snorkel or dive the reefs around Dry Tortugas NP in spring and scan open blue water as well as reef edges. Mantas often feed near the surface in calm conditions and may approach divers curiously.

animals.name: Whale Shark
animals.behavior_notes: Whale sharks aggregrate in warm Gulf and Atlantic waters during summer months, often feeding near the surface on fish spawn and plankton blooms. They are slow-moving filter feeders and are generally docile around snorkelers and divers.
animals.viewing_tips: Book a live-aboard dive charter out of Key West or Miami that specifically targets whale shark aggregations. Sightings are not guaranteed; late June around full moon spawning events offer the best odds.

animals.name: Hammerhead Shark
animals.behavior_notes: Scalloped hammerheads move into shallow warm waters around Florida's southern reefs and offshore banks during summer to feed and pup. They are often seen in schools near reef drop-offs at dawn and dusk.
animals.viewing_tips: Join a guided dive or snorkel trip from Key West to Dry Tortugas reefs; early morning dives yield the best sighting chances. Remain calm and avoid erratic movements to keep sharks relaxed and in the area.

animals.name: Great White Shark
animals.behavior_notes: Great white sharks aggregate around the northern Channel Islands in fall and winter, drawn by large populations of elephant seals and pinnipeds hauling out to breed. They are most active near the surface during morning hours when hunting pinnipeds.
animals.viewing_tips: Book a shark dive expedition from Ventura or Oxnard with a licensed operator offering cage or surface viewing near Anacapa and Santa Rosa islands. Fall months offer the highest encounter rates as seal populations peak on the beaches.

animals.name: Bull Shark
animals.behavior_notes: Bull sharks are uniquely adapted to freshwater and regularly penetrate the brackish rivers and estuaries of the Everglades during summer wet season. They are most active at dawn and dusk, hunting fish, turtles, and other prey in murky shallow water.
animals.viewing_tips: Take a ranger-led boat tour along the Shark River Slough or hire a guided kayak outfitter in the Ten Thousand Islands area for the best sighting opportunities. Do not wade or swim in Everglades waterways where bull sharks are present year-round.

animals.name: Coelacanth
parks: None — not found in US waters
animals.behavior_notes: The coelacanth is a critically endangered deep-sea fish found only in the Indian Ocean and Indonesian waters, not in US territory. It was long thought extinct until rediscovered in 1938 and remains one of the rarest fish on Earth.
animals.viewing_tips: Coelacanths cannot be viewed in the wild in the United States. Your best opportunity is visiting the Florida Museum of Natural History in Gainesville, FL, or the American Museum of Natural History in New York City, which have preserved specimens on display.

animals.name: Brook Trout
animals.behavior_notes: Brook trout are native to cold, clear Appalachian streams and are most active in spring and fall when water temperatures are optimal between 50–65°F. During these seasons they feed aggressively on aquatic insects near stream surfaces, making them highly visible in shallow riffles.
animals.viewing_tips: Hike along the upper reaches of Shenandoah's tributary streams such as the Rapidan River or Hogcamp Branch, where brook trout can be spotted in clear pools from streamside rocks. Early morning visits before foot traffic increases yield the clearest water and most active fish.

animals.name: Largemouth Bass
animals.behavior_notes: Largemouth bass are most active and visible during spring spawning season when males move into shallow vegetated areas to build and guard nests, often in water less than 3 feet deep. At dawn and dusk they aggressively strike at prey near the surface, making them easy to spot.
animals.viewing_tips: Bring polarized sunglasses and walk slowly along the vegetated shorelines of Big Thicket's Village Creek or Lance Rosier Unit ponds to spot bass in the shallows. Calm, warm mornings in April offer the best combination of clear water and active surface feeding behavior.

animals.name: Monarch Butterfly
parks: Monarch Butterfly Biosphere Reserve corridor / Natural Bridges State Beach
animals.behavior_notes: Monarchs migrate south and west in late summer and fall, forming large roost clusters in trees at night and nectaring heavily on milkweed and wildflowers during the day. Peak migration corridors include the Texas coast and California's central coast.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit coastal California roost sites like Natural Bridges State Beach in October–November or watch migration along the Texas coast in September. Look for clusters of orange wings hanging from eucalyptus or pine trees.

animals.name: Eastern Tiger Swallowtail
animals.behavior_notes: Eastern Tiger Swallowtails are active from spring through early fall, visiting flower gardens, forest edges, and roadsides to nectar. Males frequently puddle along muddy stream banks to obtain minerals.
animals.viewing_tips: Look for them nectaring on joe-pye weed, milkweed, and butterfly bush along sunny forest edges and garden paths. Mud puddles and stream crossings are great spots to find multiple males gathered together.

animals.name: Painted Lady Butterfly
animals.behavior_notes: Painted Ladies are highly migratory and nomadic, with populations expanding dramatically in years of high rainfall that boost host plant growth. They nectar on thistles, asters, and composites in open meadows and disturbed habitats.
animals.viewing_tips: Thistle patches in open fields and roadsides are the best places to spot Painted Ladies. Population numbers vary greatly by year — high rainfall years in the Southwest can produce spectacular migrations visible from late spring onward.

animals.name: Luna Moth
animals.behavior_notes: Luna Moths are nocturnal and most active on warm, humid nights in spring. Adults do not feed — they live only to mate — making their appearance brief and often near outdoor lights or along forest edges.
animals.viewing_tips: Check exterior lights on warm spring nights, especially near hardwood forests with walnut, hickory, or sweet gum. Leave porch lights on and check screens and walls around dawn when moths are winding down their nightly activity.

animals.name: Cecropia Moth
animals.behavior_notes: Cecropia Moths are North America's largest native moth, flying only at night to locate mates using pheromones. Like other giant silk moths, adults have no functional mouthparts and live only 1–2 weeks as adults solely to reproduce.
animals.viewing_tips: Attract Cecropia Moths by leaving outdoor lights on near wooded areas on warm, calm nights in late spring and early summer. Check tree trunks and shrubs at dawn — freshly emerged adults often rest motionless for hours after a night of flying.

animals.name: American Bumble Bee
animals.behavior_notes: American Bumble Bees forage on native wildflowers from late morning through afternoon, visiting clover, coneflowers, and milkweed in open grasslands and prairies. This species has experienced severe population declines and is now a candidate for federal endangered species listing.
animals.viewing_tips: Search native wildflower patches in tallgrass prairies and restored meadows, particularly on warm sunny mornings. Distinguish the American Bumble Bee by its yellow and black pattern and relatively large size compared to other bumble bees in the same area.

animals.name: Firefly (Eastern)
animals.behavior_notes: Eastern Fireflies begin flashing shortly after sunset on warm, humid evenings, with males flying low and flashing in species-specific patterns to attract perched females. Peak activity typically occurs 30–90 minutes after sunset when temperatures remain above 60°F.
animals.viewing_tips: The synchronous firefly display in Great Smoky Mountains NP is world-famous — visit in mid-June and enter the NPS lottery for shuttle passes to the Elkmont Campground area. For general firefly viewing, find open meadows near forest edges and water on calm, warm evenings.

animals.name: Periodical Cicada
animals.behavior_notes: Periodical Cicadas emerge en masse every 13 or 17 years depending on brood, filling deciduous forests with deafening song as males call to attract females. They emerge when soil temperatures reach 64°F and are most active and vocal during warm midday hours.
animals.viewing_tips: Check emergence schedules for specific broods online — Brood XIII and Brood XIX last co-emerged in 2024, a rare dual emergence. Walk deciduous forest trails in late May and listen for the loud, sustained chorus; cicadas will be visible on nearly every tree trunk and shrub during emergence years.

animals.name: Dragonfly (Common Green Darner)
animals.behavior_notes: Common Green Darners are one of the few migratory dragonflies in North America, forming large swarms along the Atlantic coast in late summer and fall as they move southward. They are active hunters of flying insects over open water, fields, and wetlands during daylight hours.
animals.viewing_tips: Watch for migrating swarms along coastal dunes, fields, and barrier islands in September and October, particularly after cold fronts push them south. Ponds and wetland edges are excellent spots to observe hunting darners hovering and darting after prey throughout the summer months.

animals.name: Praying Mantis
animals.behavior_notes: Praying Mantises reach adult size by late summer and become most visible in August through October as they hunt actively among tall grasses, shrubs, and garden vegetation. Females grow noticeably large by fall and may be spotted waiting motionlessly in flowers to ambush visiting pollinators.
animals.viewing_tips: Scan tall wildflower patches, goldenrod, and shrubby vegetation slowly and carefully — mantises rely on camouflage and will freeze when approached. Gardens and meadow edges are productive spots; look for their triangular heads and grasping forelegs angled forward on stems.

animals.name: Walking Stick
animals.behavior_notes: Walking sticks are most active at night during summer months, feeding on leaves of oaks, maples, and shrubs. They rely on exceptional camouflage and remain motionless during the day, making them very difficult to spot without deliberate searching.
animals.viewing_tips: Scan leafy shrubs and low tree branches slowly at dusk with a red-light flashlight to avoid startling them. Look for unusual twig-like shapes that do not match nearby branch angles.

animals.name: Tarantula (Desert Blonde)
animals.behavior_notes: Male desert blonde tarantulas roam widely in late summer and fall in search of mates, making them far more visible during this period than at any other time of year. Females remain closer to their burrows and are rarely seen above ground.
animals.viewing_tips: Drive desert roads slowly at dusk in southern Arizona during September and October — males crossing roads are the most reliable sighting method. Look for their distinctive tan-and-brown coloring against dark pavement.

animals.name: Black Widow Spider
animals.behavior_notes: Black widows are most active in warm months and typically build irregular, tangled webs in dark, sheltered areas such as rock crevices, log undersides, and man-made structures. They are nocturnal hunters and more readily observed after dark.
animals.viewing_tips: Use a UV flashlight at night — black widow webs fluoresce and spiders are easier to spot. Check the undersides of rocks, logs, and picnic table ledges carefully, and always wear gloves when reaching into dark crevices.

animals.name: American Horseshoe Crab
parks: Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area
animals.behavior_notes: American horseshoe crabs mass-spawn on sandy beaches during high tides around full and new moons in May and June, particularly along Delaware Bay — one of the largest such gatherings in the world. Males crowd around females as they come ashore to lay eggs just below the sand surface.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit Delaware Bay beaches such as Slaughter Beach or Pickering Beach on nights with a full or new moon in late May for the best spectacle. Arrive at high tide and walk the waterline carefully to avoid stepping on spawning animals.

animals.name: Giant Water Bug
animals.behavior_notes: Giant water bugs are active predatory insects found in slow-moving freshwater ponds, marshes, and streams, where they ambush prey including small fish and tadpoles. Warmer spring and early summer months coincide with increased activity and dispersal flights at dusk toward lights near water.
animals.viewing_tips: Check the edges of lit areas near ponds and marshes at dusk, as giant water bugs are attracted to lights during dispersal flights. Slowly scan shallow marsh edges with a flashlight for the large, flattened brown insects resting just below the water surface.

animals.name: Pacific Octopus
animals.behavior_notes: Pacific octopuses are most active during cooler months when they forage along rocky intertidal and subtidal zones. They emerge from dens at dawn and dusk to hunt crabs, clams, and small fish.
animals.viewing_tips: Look in rocky tide pools and kelp forest edges during low tides. Snorkeling or scuba diving in calm nearshore waters of the Pacific Northwest offers the best sightings.

animals.name: Giant Pacific Octopus
animals.behavior_notes: Giant Pacific Octopuses are most visible in late winter and early spring when males venture out to seek mates. This is the largest octopus species in the world and can span up to 14 feet across.
animals.viewing_tips: Scuba diving in Puget Sound or Alaskan coastal waters offers the best chance of encounters. Look under rocky ledges and in submarine caves at depths of 30–100 feet.

animals.name: Bioluminescent Dinoflagellates
animals.behavior_notes: Bioluminescent dinoflagellates bloom in warm, calm coastal waters during summer months, producing vivid blue-green light when disturbed. Blooms are most spectacular on moonless nights in sheltered bays and lagoons.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit on a new moon night and allow 20 minutes for your eyes to adjust to darkness. Kayaking through warm lagoons like Mosquito Lagoon in Florida or bioluminescent bays in Puerto Rico produces the most dramatic light displays.

animals.name: Jellyfish (Moon)
animals.behavior_notes: Moon jellyfish aggregate in large surface blooms during summer, drifting with tidal currents in bays and harbors. They are most visible from docks, kayaks, or during low tide along calm shorelines.
animals.viewing_tips: Look for translucent white bells near the water's surface from piers, kayaks, or glass-bottom boat tours. Bar Harbor, ME and calm Pacific Coast harbors are excellent viewing spots in summer.

animals.name: Dungeness Crab
animals.behavior_notes: Dungeness crabs are most active in late fall and winter when they migrate into shallower estuarine waters to molt and mate. They can be spotted foraging along sandy and muddy coastal bottoms during low tides.
animals.viewing_tips: Wade into shallow estuaries or rocky intertidal areas during extreme low tides along the Washington and Oregon coasts. Bring a flashlight for early morning searches in tidal channels and seagrass beds.

animals.name: American Lobster
animals.behavior_notes: American lobsters move into shallower waters during summer months as water temperatures rise, making them more accessible to divers and snorkelers. They are primarily nocturnal, emerging from rocky dens after dark to scavenge.
animals.viewing_tips: Scuba diving or snorkeling along rocky subtidal ledges off the coast of Maine offers the best wild sightings. Night dives are most productive; look for antennae protruding from crevices and under boulders at depths of 10–50 feet.

animals.name: Coral (Elkhorn)
animals.behavior_notes: Elkhorn coral is a critically endangered species found in shallow Florida reef tracts at depths of 1–15 feet. Summer spawning events occur in August on nights following a full moon, releasing bundles of eggs and sperm into the water column.
animals.viewing_tips: Snorkeling tours at Biscayne National Park and Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary offer guided access to remaining elkhorn coral colonies. Populations are severely reduced — treat this as a rare, conservation-sensitive sighting.

animals.name: Coral (Brain)
animals.behavior_notes: Brain corals are slow-growing, dome-shaped reef builders most visible during the calm, clear spring months in the Caribbean. Their complex grooved surface serves as habitat for small reef fish, shrimp, and invertebrates.
animals.viewing_tips: Snorkeling along the underwater trail at Buck Island Reef National Monument in the US Virgin Islands provides excellent close-up views of brain coral. Spring offers the clearest water visibility before summer storm season begins.

animals.name: Sea Anemone
animals.behavior_notes: Sea anemones are abundant in Pacific Coast tide pools and are most active and fully extended during spring low tides when water temperatures are cool and calm. They retract their tentacles when touched or exposed to air at low tide.
animals.viewing_tips: Visit tide pools at Rialto Beach or Ruby Beach in Olympic National Park during minus tides in spring. Arrive within an hour before low tide and look in shaded, water-filled crevices and rock faces where anemones cluster in colorful colonies.

animals.name: Starfish (Sunflower Sea Star)
animals.behavior_notes: The sunflower sea star is critically endangered due to sea star wasting disease and is now extremely rare throughout its former range. Remaining individuals are found in deeper, cooler subtidal waters along the Pacific Coast during fall and winter.
animals.viewing_tips: Sightings are exceptionally rare — this species has declined by over 90% since 2013. Report any confirmed sighting to the Pacific Rocky Intertidal Monitoring network. Scuba diving in deeper kelp forest habitats off the Channel Islands offers the best remaining chance.

animals.name: Pacific Sea Nettle
animals.behavior_notes: Pacific Sea Nettles bloom in large aggregations along the California coast during late summer as warm surface currents concentrate nutrients. They drift passively with currents, trailing long tentacles to capture zooplankton and small fish.
animals.viewing_tips: Look for golden-orange bell-shaped jellies drifting near the surface from kayaks or glass-bottom boat tours around Channel Islands. Calm, sunny days with minimal wind offer the clearest water visibility.

animals.name: Mantis Shrimp
animals.behavior_notes: Mantis shrimp are most active during crepuscular hours in spring, emerging from burrows in shallow reef rubble and seagrass beds to hunt prey. Warmer spring waters increase their foraging activity and territorial displays.
animals.viewing_tips: Snorkel or dive the shallow coral rubble zones at Biscayne at dawn or dusk and look carefully in small crevices and burrow openings for their distinctive colorful bodies. Avoid reaching into holes, as their strike can cause serious injury.
